items.text:
**Problem:** Cross-session memory: notebook tab + cross-session announce + /recall

**Theme A** from `_myco_/research/ai-team-collaboration.md` — the leverage layer for "two sessions on the same project shouldn't re-discover the same finding".

**Subfeatures:**
- `_myco_/notebook.md` — free-form team scratchpad, new artifact tab next to Plan/Arch/Test. Append-only by default; AI is encouraged to write findings here.
- **Cross-session announce** — fs.watch on `_myco_/` per project; when one live AgentSession writes a notebook entry or plan item, other live sessions for the same project get a toast.
- **`/recall <topic>`** slash command — greps `_myco_/*` for matches across chat + notebook + plan comments, top-N hits inline.
- Optional Phase 1.5: session "merge" — open another session's historical transcript inline (read-only).

**Source:** `_myco_/research/ai-team-collaboration.md` (theme A).

**Problem:** Approval queue + trust ladder + audit log

**Theme B** — async review queue for high-stakes AI actions.

**Subfeatures:**
- Per-tool trust tier extended: allow / deny / **queue-for-review** (new). Queued tool calls pause the request without blocking the agent loop; chat continues.
- **Review queue tab** (new chrome icon) — shows pending reviews across all the user's sessions: who requested (session label + AI persona), what (tool + input summary + risk reason), when, approve/reject inline.
- **Reviewer routing** — plan items can carry `reviewer: alice`; tool calls in their scope route to alice's queue specifically.
- **`_myco_/audit.jsonl`** — append (reviewer, action, tool, hash, ts) on every approve/reject. OAuth identity = attributable.
- **Time-bounded auto-trust** — "trust this AI with bash for 30 min" or "let it spend $5 of credits before pausing".

Unlocks "team gives AI more agency without losing oversight". Today every team has to either fully trust + risk wreckage, or fully gate + lose throughput.

**Source:** `_myco_/research/ai-team-collaboration.md` (theme B).

**Problem:** AI ↔ human question routing: /ask @user with SLA + topic subscriptions

**Theme C** — when AI hits ambiguity, route the question to a specific human (not OS-broadcast to everyone).

**Subfeatures:**
- **`/ask @user <question>`** as a first-class agent-event (the SDK's AskUserQuestion tool surface OR a /slash). Named user gets high-priority chip + OS notification + (later) email. Others see a muted "alice has a question from session X" line.
- **Follow-the-thread answer** — when alice answers, the reply is posted back to the originating AI session as a `chat` frame with `meta.replyTo: <ask-id>`. AI sees it as conversational input.
- **SLA / fallback** — `/ask @alice (default after 1h: just use prisma)` lets AI proceed with a sensible default. Avoids "AI blocked for 8 hours" pathology.
- **Topic subscriptions** — `/subscribe auth` or `/subscribe src/auth/**` in a user's profile. When ANY session's chat mentions auth or touches a matching file, that user is added to the interested roster + digested.

First version of `/ask` is ~50 LOC; subscriptions are another ~50 LOC scanner on top of the existing event stream.

**Source:** `_myco_/research/ai-team-collaboration.md` (theme C).

**Problem:** Team observability dashboard: /team + cost rollup + stuck-session + replay

**Theme D** — what every AI on your team is doing right now, and what it has spent.

**Subfeatures:**
- **`/team` route** (top-level, not per-session) — every session for users in the allowlist, with live status (running / waiting-on-perm / idle / dead), latest activity, current cost burn, and 1-line agent-state.
- **Cost rollup** — sum `total_cost_usd` per user + per day + per project. Server tracks via existing turn_result events; UI shows a small bar chart.
- **Stuck-session detector** — permission_request >5 min OR awaiting_claude >2 min with no chrome activity → yellow ⚠ chip on /team dashboard.
- **Replay scrubber** — open any session's transcript at a point in time. Already have the data (rec.chat + events.jsonl with seq #); needs a read-only time-travel renderer. Higher effort — recommend Phase 4.

**Source:** `_myco_/research/ai-team-collaboration.md` (theme D).

**Problem:** Async handoff: /handoff @user + resume summary + steering pass + inbox

**Theme E** — clean async handoff between teammates on the same session.

**Subfeatures:**
- **`/handoff @bob <one-liner>`** — bookmark current session state with a special chat row (kind:'handoff'), ping bob, scroll to handoff marker on his first attach.
- **Resume summary** — when a teammate attaches to a session idle >15 min, show a "Resume" banner: "Last activity 2h ago. AI completed 3 turns since you left. Click to summarise." Click → AI generates a 5-bullet recap as a chat-msg.
- **Steering pass** — explicit "you have the wheel" / "give me the wheel" so two humans don't simultaneously type at the same AI. Whoever has it sees a 🎯 driving chip, others get 👀 watching + read-only input.
- **Inbox** — top-level item showing pending /ask + /handoff + review-queue entries directed at the current user across all their sessions. Single funnel for "what needs my attention".

**Source:** `_myco_/research/ai-team-collaboration.md` (theme E).

**Problem:** Adopt two-pass code review: AI first-pass automation, human second-pass on every merged PR

**Practice change** (no myco code). Source: `_myco_/research/ai-team-collaboration.md` §5.2.

**What:** Every PR gets two passes. (1) AI first-pass — formatting, obvious bugs, missing tests, alignment with `_myco_/architecture.md`. Automate this (GitHub Action calling the SDK, or a /loop tick). (2) Human second-pass — design judgment, edge cases, "does this make sense for our users".

**Anti-pattern to avoid:** rubber-stamping. The first time you skip the human pass, the result will be fine. The tenth time, you ship something embarrassing.

**Done when:** PR template includes both checkboxes; a GitHub Action posts AI first-pass comments; team agrees the human-second-pass is mandatory for merge.

**Problem:** Standardize commit message format: what + why + what-this-leaves-undone

**Practice change**. Source: §5.1 ("Write for two readers").

**Why:** AI loads commit messages as primary context. Vague messages ("fix bug") starve future AI sessions and human reviewers equally. The team's commit messages from 2026-05-17 (e.g. `chat: re-apply seq-based ordering minimally — fixes "agent reply merged with previous replies"`) are the model — concise subject + body that explains the user-visible symptom + what changed + what was left undone.

**Done when:** new section added to CLAUDE.md best-practices template (rule 7?) covering the 3-line minimum (what / why / un-done); pre-commit hook nudges authors who skip the body on diffs > N lines.

**Problem:** No team-shared policy on which tools should auto-allow, queue-for-review, or auto-deny. AI trust today is scattered across per-session `permissions.js` entries — inconsistent across teammates, no rationale documented, no review cadence.

**Expected:** A single `_myco_/policy/ai-allowed-tools.md` documents the per-tool tier + rationale + quarterly review. Server enforces against this file (via fr-16) instead of each session re-deriving its own rules.

**Starting tiers:**

| Tier | Tools |
|---|---|
| **Allow** | `Read`, `Glob`, `Grep`, `WebFetch` (docs), `Bash` read-only (`git status`, `ls`, `cat`) |
| **Queue → promote per-path after observation** | `Edit`, `Write` on source files |
| **Queue** | `Bash` mutating: `git push`, `npm install`, `rm`, `docker` |
| **Deny** | Anything touching prod creds, deploy keys, customer DBs |

**Done when:** policy file lands at `_myco_/policy/ai-allowed-tools.md` + quarterly review on calendar + fr-16 server enforcement gates against this file (until fr-16 ships, this is documentation only).

**Depends on:** fr-16 (review queue + trust ladder feature).

**Source:** `_myco_/research/ai-team-collaboration.md` §5.1 — "Calibrate AI trust per tool, not per session."

meta.originalText:
**Problem:** Define + document the per-tool AI trust ladder for this team

**Practice change** + light artifact. Source: §5.1 ("Calibrate AI trust per tool, not per session").

**What:** New file `_myco_/policy/ai-allowed-tools.md` documenting per-tool tier (allow / queue-for-review / deny) and the rationale. Reviewed quarterly.

**Starting heuristic:**
- `Read`, `Glob`, `Grep`, `WebFetch` (docs) → allow
- `Bash` (read-only commands like `git status`, `ls`, `cat`) → allow
- `Edit`, `Write` on source files → queue at first, promote to allow per-path after observation
- `Bash` (mutating: `git push`, `npm install`, `rm`, `docker`) → queue
- Anything touching prod creds, deploy keys, customer DBs → deny

**Depends on:** fr-16 (review queue + trust ladder feature). Without fr-16 the file is documentation only; with fr-16 it becomes the actual policy the server enforces.

items.text:
**Problem:** Pilot AI-guided onboarding for the next new contributor

**Practice change**. Source: §5.2 ("Onboarding flips").

**What:** Designate the next new contributor to start with an AI tour guide. Pre-load a tour-guide persona into the project's CLAUDE.md (or a separate file the SDK loads). The persona's job on Day 1: walk through the codebase, explain conventions, point at gotchas in `_myco_/notebook.md`. Week 1: pair with the new hire on their first small fix. Month 1: hand them their own plan items to dispatch.

**Done when:** the pilot completes; we have a tour-guide persona refined into a reusable template; we document time-to-first-merge for the AI-guided vs control flow.

**Tracks:** new-contributor TTFM (time to first merge) as a metric.

**Problem:** Add an AI-state question to the daily standup template

**Practice change**. Source: §5.2 ("Standups become AI-state syncs").

**What:** The 15-minute morning sync gets a fourth question: "What is your AI session blocked on, and who can unblock it?". If alice's session has been awaiting `/ask @bob` for 6 hours, that's the standup item.

**Artifact:** the `/team` dashboard (fr-18) is the standup screen-share. Open it on the shared screen, walk through any yellow ⚠ chips (stuck-session detector).

**Done when:** standup template updated; team norm sticks for two consecutive sprints; fr-18 lands and we're actually screen-sharing the dashboard.

**Problem:** Set up team metrics: 7-day revert rate, time-in-queue, cost-per-feature, plan-item test coverage

**Practice change** + small tooling. Source: §5.5 ("New metrics").

**Metrics:**
1. *7-day revert rate* — diffs reverted within 7 days of merge. Proxy for AI's confidently-wrong rate. Compute from `git log --since=7d --grep=revert`.
2. *Time-in-review-queue* — how long do permission requests sit before approve/reject. From fr-16's audit log.
3. *Cost per shipped feature* — sum `total_cost_usd` from turn_results for sessions tagged to each plan-item run. From fr-18 cost rollup.
4. *New-contributor TTFM* — days from first commit to first merged PR.
5. *Plan-item test coverage* — fraction of closed plan items that landed with a test. Heuristic: search the closeout commit message for the test filename.

**Done when:** a `./scripts/metrics.sh` exists that prints all five numbers; posted to chat weekly via a /loop tick.

comments.text: add a metrics icon beside the test/plan/arch/file for this

items.text:
**Problem:** Adopt smaller-atomic-commit discipline; pre-commit hook flags >500-line single-concept commits

**Practice change**. Source: §5.1 ("Commit smaller, more often").

**Why:** AI reasons better about a small atomic diff than a 1500-line "weekly sync" commit. The same discipline that makes human review easier makes AI's mental model of the codebase converge faster.

**What:** Pre-commit hook that warns (not blocks) when a commit touches >500 lines AND looks like a single concept (no `feat:`+`refactor:`+`docs:` markers). Easy override for legitimate big diffs.

**Done when:** hook installed in the project's `.git/hooks/`; documented in CLAUDE.md; team agrees it's a warning, never a block.

**Problem:** Architectural Decision Records: _myco_/decisions/ + /adr slash command

**Feature**, supports §5.2 ("Decision logs become a team artifact").

**What:** New directory `_myco_/decisions/` holding ADR files (one per decision, named `YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>.md`). Format: Context / Decision / Consequences / Alternatives-rejected.

**Slash command:** `/adr <title>` — AI drafts an ADR from the current chat thread's discussion, opens it as a new file for human edit, commits + pushes when approved.

**Auto-read:** every AgentSession on this project reads `_myco_/decisions/*.md` into its initial context (lazily — only the most recent 5 or those tagged with @-mentions of files in the current change set). When AI proposes a rejected approach, it knows to flag the relevant ADR.

**Done when:** directory + template exists; `/adr` slash command works end-to-end; SDK setting auto-loads ADRs.

comments.text: should there be a way to display the architecture decisions in the architecture view?

items.text:
**Problem:** _myco_/policy/ artifacts: ai-allowed-tools, ai-deny-list, review-routing

**Feature**, supports §5.5 ("AI policy as a real artifact").

**What:** New directory `_myco_/policy/` holding three version-controlled artifacts:
- `ai-allowed-tools.md` — what AI may run unattended (per-tool tier from td above)
- `ai-deny-list.md` — never-touched paths (prod creds, customer DBs, deploy keys). Parsed by canUseTool / PreToolUse hook.
- `review-routing.md` — who reviews what. Parsed by the review-queue routing logic from fr-16.

**Why:** policy belongs in git, not in a Slack DM or a memo. Code-review-able, version-traceable, attributable.

**Depends on:** fr-16 (review queue) for the routing artifact to do anything; the deny-list and allowed-tools artifacts can ship independently.

**Problem:** AI tour guide persona + new-contributor walkthrough mode

**Feature**, supports §5.2 ("Onboarding flips").

**What:** A reusable persona template (loaded into the SDK's system prompt) that turns an AgentSession into an onboarding tour guide. Reads `_myco_/architecture.md`, `_myco_/decisions/*.md`, `_myco_/notebook.md`, and offers an interactive walkthrough: "Where would you like to start? (a) auth flow, (b) data layer, (c) deploy pipeline …". Knows the project conventions, points at common gotchas, suggests the new contributor's first small fix.

**UI:** a new "Onboard a teammate" button in the session-spawn modal that creates a session pre-loaded with the tour-guide persona + a fresh chat with the welcome message.

**Done when:** persona template lives at `_myco_/onboarding/tour-guide.md` (overridable per project); spawn-modal button exists; the pilot in the td above runs through it.

**Problem:** Re-entering a session via the back icon leaves the chat window deactivated, blocking user input.
**Expected:** The chat window should always be active when viewing a session.
**Actual:** Clicking the icon to return to the session card and then reopening the same session deactivates the chat window.

comments.text:
**Fixed.** Mobile flow that produced the symptom:

1. User in session's chat → `state.chatPaneVisible = true`
2. Taps back icon `#btn-expand` → `setSidebar(false)` fires; on mobile (≤900px) line at `app.js:1348` cascades: `if (!collapsed && window.innerWidth <= 900) setChatPane(false)` → chat pane hidden
3. Taps the SAME session card → `openSession()` hits the re-tap early-return at `app.js:1153-1156` because `state.activeId === id` and the WS is still open
4. **Pre-fix:** the early-return only called `setSidebar(true)` to collapse the now-open sidebar — it never restored the chat pane. Result: neither sidebar nor chat visible, input effectively deactivated.

**Fix** (`web/public/app.js openSession` re-tap branch): added `setChatPane(true)` immediately after `setSidebar(true)`. No-op when the pane is already visible (idempotent); restores the pane in the back-icon-then-re-tap path.

**Regression test**: `test/bug-12-reentry-restores-chatpane.test.js` — 4 cases (3 static-grep guards on the re-tap branch + setChatPane polarity contract + 1 end-to-end behavior simulation of the mobile flow with fake state). Wired into `test.sh` right after the fr-51 entry. Full sweep: 50/50 individual test files green; `test.sh` itself bails on the known-environmental BusyBox `grep -P` issue at `test_cache_busters` (unrelated).

**Awaiting deploy** — fix is in commit <see git log>. Live myco is pre-fix; verify on prod after the next `./deploy.sh`.

meta.originalText: click the icon to go back to session card and then go back to the same session would case the chat window be disactivated. The chat window should be always active

items.text:
**Problem:** myco isn't installable as a PWA. No home-screen icon, mobile tabs get reaped on memory pressure, and the SW foundation for Web Push (`fr-7`, `fr-8`, `td-18`) doesn't exist yet.

**Expected:** Tap-to-install (Chrome/Edge/iOS-A2HS), home-screen icon, standalone window, fast first paint, and a SW that unblocks backgrounded notifications.

**Scope (in order):**

1. **Service worker** `web/public/sw.js` — register from `app.js`, cache vendor bundle cache-first, leave `app.js` / `index.html` / `/auth/*` / `/sessions/*` / `/attach/*` / `/chat/history` / `/logs` network-only. Cache name keyed by deploy SHA → atomic invalidation.
2. **Multi-size icons** — `hetu-192.png` / `-512.png` / `-512-maskable.png` from existing transparent `hetu.png` via `scripts/generate-pwa-icons.mjs` (jimp). Manifest update. Chrome requires ≥192 PNG for install prompt.
3. **Install button** — `#btn-install` in sidebar header, captures `beforeinstallprompt`, hides in standalone mode. iOS fallback tooltip with share-sheet A2HS instructions (iOS lacks `beforeinstallprompt`).
4. **Splash + status bar** — `apple-touch-startup-image` × iOS screen-size matrix, generated by same icon script. Android picks up from existing `theme_color`/`background_color`.
5. **Atomic-update discipline (MANDATORY)** — `skipWaiting()` + `clients.claim()` on activate, server stamps build SHA on `/auth/check`, client offers 1-tap refresh on mismatch. Without this, a SW makes deploys WORSE (cached stale code). If item 5 is dropped, item 1 must be dropped too.
6. **Tests** — extend `test_pwa_icon` with `test_service_worker`: SW exists, register call in `app.js`, manifest has ≥192 icon, `sw.js` served `no-cache`, `clients.claim()` present, cache name version-templated.

**Out of scope:** offline session-shell read (mostly theater for server-driven app), background sync, share-target. Web Push lives in `fr-7`/`fr-8`.

**Unblocks:** `fr-7`, `fr-8`, `td-18` (all blocked on a registered SW).

**Effort:** ~½ day.

**Problem:** Plan-item mutations today happen only via the chat-driven LLM editing `plan.json` with the Edit tool, or via `mcp__myco__add_plan_items` (add-only). No scriptable surface for close/merge/mark-stale. Also no "someone proposed an action, awaiting human approval" concept — closures happen silently or not at all.

**Expected:** CLI mutation script + proposal data model + UI to approve/reject proposals.

**Scope (3 pieces):**

1. **`scripts/plan-tool.mjs`** — stable scriptable surface for ALL mutations. Commands: `close <id> --reason`, `merge <from> --into <to>`, `mark-stale <id>`, `reopen <id>`, `add-proposal <id> --action --reason --source`, `approve-proposal <pid>`, `reject-proposal <pid>`. File-locked reads/writes of `_myco_/plan.json`. Pure CLI, no LLM, idempotent, testable. Each action stamps a claude comment with `meta.kind:'mutation'` for audit.

2. **Proposal data model** — each item gets optional `proposals: [{ id, action, args, reason, source, ts, status }]` where `status` ∈ `pending|approved|rejected`. `approve-proposal` flips status + executes the action; `reject-proposal` flips status only.

3. **UI surface** — plan-item row shows "📥 N proposals" badge when any pending exists. Click expands inline panel listing each (action/reason/source) with Approve/Reject buttons. Routes `POST /sessions/:id/plan/item/:id/proposal/:pid/{approve|reject}` shell out to plan-tool.mjs.

**Tests:** `test/plan-tool.test.js` covering each command × fixture (idempotency, file-lock, audit-comment). UI smoke for the panel + approve flow.

**Unblocks:** Companion housekeeping-policy FR filed next. Also unblocks future LLM-suggests-merge + bulk-close flows.

**Effort:** ~1 day. CLI is load-bearing; data model + UI follow same day.

**Problem:** Stale plan items accumulate silently. This session found + closed 5 items (`bug-11`, `bug-7`, `fr-6`, `fr-7`, `td-11`) implemented in past commits but never marked done — the /run mechanism stamps `ranAt` + posts a comment, but `done:true` is manual. Same staleness applies to duplicates + abandoned items.

**Expected:** Periodic cron scans the plan + emits proposals (via the `fr-24` substrate) so the user reviews + approves with one click instead of doing manual archaeology.

**Scope:**

1. **Cron** — weekly tick (parallel to existing diag /loop), runs `scripts/plan-housekeep.mjs` against `_myco_/plan.json`. Posts one-line `🧹 [housekeep]` summary to chat per run.

2. **Rules (each calls `plan-tool.mjs add-proposal`):**
   - **(a) Closure-candidate** — item has a comment matching `/^✓ (success|closed|done)/i` AND `done:false` → propose `close` with reason quoting the comment + cited commit. Catches the 5 items I closed manually this session.
   - **(b) Stale-candidate** — `addedAt < now - 90d` AND 0 voters AND 0 user-comments AND `done:false` → propose `mark-stale`.
   - **(c) Duplicate-candidate** — title-similarity above threshold via local trigram match (no LLM in v1) → propose `merge` with reason "high title similarity to <other-id>".

3. **Idempotency** — each rule skips items that already have a pending proposal of the same `action`. Rejection sets `coolingOff` so the same rule won't re-propose within 30d.

4. **Tests:** `test/plan-housekeep.test.js` — fixture plan.json triggering each rule once; second run is a no-op.

**Depends on:** `fr-24` shipping first.

**Effort:** ~½ day after `fr-24`. Ship rule (a) first — clearest signal + most user-visible payoff; (b)/(c) follow.

**Problem:** Creating a new session leaves the plan view showing stale data from the previously selected session.
**Expected:** Adding a new session initializes the plan view with that session's own data (empty/default state).
**Actual:** The plan view continues to display whatever was shown before the new session was added.

meta.originalText: adding new session will not initialize the plan view with session specific data. it will show whatever was shown before

items.text:
**Problem:** Chrome batch messages are only visible to the session owner, not other participants.
**Expected:** Batch messages should be visible to all users in the session.
**Actual:** Only the owner sees them.

comments.text:
**Verified fixed — re-closing.**

The bug-13 fix shipped in **9394e2c** (2026-05-18, "viewer: bug-13 — full event fan-out to share-link viewers"). The prior closure at 9d0e93f was reverted at 8cc4c65 with no commit-body explanation — re-verifying from scratch:

**Code wiring (server)** — `server/src/attach.js attachViewerWebSocket` (line 1214):
- `session.on('agent-event', onAgentEvent)` — viewer subscribes to chrome-batch events
- `session.on('exit', onExit)` — viewer sees session-ended
- `_shipAgentReplay(...)` on attach — viewer gets recent chrome batches, not only events going forward
- `session.off('agent-event', ...)` + `session.off('exit', ...)` in close handler — no listener leak across reconnects
- Frame shape: `{ t: 'agent-event', event }` — byte-identical to the owner path
- Catch-up mode (`?afterSeq=N`): `_shipAgentReplay(session, ws, ..., afterSeq)` also fires for viewers

**Single emit point** — `agent-session.js:1128` `this.emit('agent-event', stamped)` broadcasts via EventEmitter to every subscriber (owner + N viewers), no per-listener filtering.

**Client (web/public/app.js)** — agent-event handler at line 1292 calls `_handleAgentFrame(msg)` unconditionally; `_appendAgentEvent` at line 3877-3879 has no `state.readOnly` guard. Verified: `grep state.readOnly` finds 11 callsites, all in queue/voting/comments/send-gate paths — none in the agent-event or chrome-batch render path.

**Regression test** — `test/viewer-agent-events.test.js` (9 cases: 4 EventEmitter-fake behavior + 5 static-grep guards on attach.js). All pass green. The static guards pin all 5 contract points above.

**Commits to attach.js since 9394e2c** (verified none regressed the viewer path): d07742d (bug-19 /qstatus), 80cf0ca (bug-19 denied-text), 72d7117 (fr-48 queue status), af45609 (fr-48 queue), c0596af (bug-21 reaper), 0eb1289 (bug-21 deadlock), c358af2 (bug-17 admin), 3bdc18f (fr-38 strict-mode), 1c7ae4c (bug-14 interrupt frame). All additive; none touched the agent-event/exit/replay block.

**If you're still seeing the symptom on prod after this closure:** prod is pre-9394e2c — the bug-13 fix is sitting in the pending-deploy queue. `./deploy.sh` will resolve it.

meta.originalText: chrome batch messages should be visible to all users in the session, right now it's only visible to owner

items.text:
**Problem:** Git commits are not attributed to the current user's GitHub identity, so authorship/accountability is lost.

**Expected:** `git commit` uses the current user's GitHub id (name + email matching their GitHub account) as the author, so every commit traces back to a real contributor.

**Actual:** Commits are made under a default/shared identity, making it impossible to tell who authored a change.

**Design:**
- Resolve the active GitHub id via `gh api user` (login + `users/{login}` for public email, fallback to `{id}+{login}@users.noreply.github.com`).
- Cache the resolved `{name, email}` per workspace; refresh on `gh auth` change.
- Before each commit, set `GIT_AUTHOR_NAME`/`GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL` (and committer) from the cache; fail fast with a clear message if `gh` is not authenticated.
- Add a pre-commit hook entry point so this runs consistently across local and agent-driven commits.

comments.text:
**Investigation (no code yet).**

### What I found in the current code + state

| Layer | Reality |
|---|---|
| `/data/auth-sessions.json` (per-token user record) | Stores `login`, `githubId`, `avatarUrl`, `expiresAt`. **`name` is `null` for ALL existing sessions** (the OAuth handler at `server/src/index.js:241-303` writes `user.name || null` and clearly `user.name` came back null from /user). **Email is not stored at all.** |
| `/data/git-tokens.json` | OAuth access tokens per (myco-user, github). 93-char tokens, `repo` scope. Usable to call `api.github.com/user` directly. |
| Existing `git config user.*` writers in repo | **None** — `grep` across `server/` finds only a test fixture in `test/files-git-decorators.test.js` and a comment in `/git` slashcmd warning about `git config --global`. **There is no production code that configures git identity per-session.** |
| Actual commits on origin/main | Today's `107575a` is by `zanqingzhang <zanqingzhang@gmail.com>`, `a8217cc` is by `kkrazy <kkrazy0630@gmail.com>`. So *some* sessions do attribute correctly — but only because each user's agent **manually** ran `git config user.email …` at some point (the CLAUDE.md context auto-injects `userEmail`, so the agent has the data and *might* set it). |

### Verdict: fr-26 is real and unsolved

Identity wiring is **happenstance**, not systematic. Whether a commit gets attributed correctly depends on whether the agent thought to run `git config user.email` in that workspace. Not safe to rely on.

### Two problems with the design as written

1. **`gh api user`** — `gh` CLI is NOT installed in the prod container (verified: `which gh` returns nothing). We'd have to either:
   - Install `gh` in the Dockerfile (extra 30MB of Go binary for a single API call), OR
   - Skip `gh` and hit `https://api.github.com/user` directly using the OAuth token we already store at `/data/git-tokens.json` (preferred — zero new dep).

2. **"Pre-commit hook entry point"** — git hooks are per-repo and only run if installed in `.git/hooks/`. They can't fix an unconfigured identity on the **first** commit (chicken-and-egg — the commit fires before the hook can do anything useful for author selection). **Setting identity at session-spawn time** is simpler and correct for the very first commit.

### The data we already have for free

For any logged-in user, auth-sessions.json gives us:
- `login` — always present
- `githubId` — always present (numeric)

That's sufficient to construct the **noreply email**:

```
<githubId>+<login>@users.noreply.github.com
```

GitHub treats this form as authoritative — it correctly attributes commits to the user's profile + avatar, and crucially **never leaks their real email**. For `kkrazy`: `65970723+kkrazy@users.noreply.github.com`.

Name is trickier — `user.name` from OAuth is null. Options:
- Use `login` as the name (simplest, always works)
- Make a one-time `api.github.com/users/{login}` call to fetch the public display name; cache in auth-sessions.json (richer but adds a network call + cache invalidation question)

### Multi-tenant correctness — pick a model

myco supports **multi-user attach** (owner + viewers + delegates can all share a session). When user A asks the agent and user B asks the agent to commit, *whose* identity does the commit carry?

| Model | Author of commit | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Session-owner identity** (Recommended) | Always the user who spawned the session | Simple, matches workspace ownership semantics | Delegate's contribution is hidden in author field |
| **Per-attach identity** | The user whose chat turn triggered the commit | Most accurate attribution | Requires pushing GIT_AUTHOR_* into the SDK env per-turn, hard to thread through Bash tool calls |
| **Co-author trailer** | Author = session owner, `Co-Authored-By:` lists the attaching user(s) | Honest about both | Needs the agent to opt in to writing the trailer — easy to forget |

Recommend: **start with session-owner identity** (covers the 95% case where attribution is owed to the spawner), document that delegates contributing significant code should add a Co-Authored-By trailer manually. Per-attach is a fr-XX follow-up if needed.

### Hook point in the code

Two natural places:

| Hook | Where | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Write `.git/config` at session spawn** | `server/src/sessions.js spawnSession()` — after `mkdir -p` of the workspace, run `git config user.email/name` against the new `.git/` (if/when one exists) | Sticky per-repo. Operator can override locally. Doesn't follow if user later does `git clone` into a sibling dir. |
| **B. Set GIT_AUTHOR_*/GIT_COMMITTER_* env in agent SDK spawn** | `server/src/agent-session.js` — when assembling `options` for `query()`, inject the env into the child process | Per-process. Covers ANY git invocation by the agent (including in sub-cloned dirs). Env vars trump config. Doesn't apply to commits the user makes from outside the agent (e.g. via `/git commit` slashcmd). |

**Recommend BOTH (belt + braces):**
- A ensures human-side commits via `/git commit` or VS Code-Remote get the right identity
- B ensures agent-side commits via Bash tool always get the right identity even in clone-derived dirs

### Test plan

`test/fr-26-git-author-identity.test.js`:
1. Build a fake auth-sessions entry for `{login:"alice", githubId:1234}`
2. Call the identity resolver → assert `{name:"alice", email:"1234+alice@users.noreply.github.com"}`
3. Spawn a temp git repo with the env vars set → `git commit --allow-empty -m t` → assert `git log -1 --format=%ae` matches
4. Cover the absence of `gh` CLI (no `gh` calls in the implementation)

### Three options for the user to pick from

**Option A — Minimal, ships in <1 hr**
- Add `server/src/git-identity.js` exporting `buildIdentity({login, githubId, name})` → `{name, email}` using the noreply form
- Hook into `agent-session.js`: inject GIT_AUTHOR_* / GIT_COMMITTER_* into `options.env` for `query()`
- Hook into `spawnSession()`: `git config user.name/email` once at session create
- One regression test (4 cases)

**Option B — Option A + fetch public name from GitHub**
- Same as A, plus a one-time `api.github.com/user` call on first session-spawn for a given login → store `name` in auth-sessions.json
- Adds a network dep (graceful fallback to `login` if API call fails)
- ~2 hr

**Option C — Option A + Co-Authored-By for attaching delegates**
- A, plus when the agent is invoked by a turn from a non-owner attacher, the commit message gets an automatic Co-Authored-By trailer
- Most accurate but invasive (touches the agent's commit-message construction path which isn't centralized)
- ~half day

**My recommendation: Option A.** Honest about its scope ("commits authored by session-owner"), zero new deps, fits the existing data model, easy to extend later. Filing fr-XX for delegate-attribution if/when needed.

Status: analysis only; awaiting Option A/B/C pick.

items.text:
**Problem:** Stop button does not actually stop the underlying process.
**Expected:** Stop button invokes the Claude Code SDK call that interrupts the current task (equivalent to the `esc` keystroke).
**Actual:** Stop button sends the literal text `'esc'` instead of the `esc` keystroke, so the underlying process keeps running.

comments.text:
✅ **Already fixed** in commit `1c7ae4c` — `stop: bug-14 — dedicated interrupt WS frame, no more chat-pollution path`.

The Stop button used to send the literal string `'esc'` through the chat-message path. Server-side that went through `handleChatPostfixes`, which DID call `session.interrupt()` for the `esc` keyword — but the user's text was ALSO persisted as a chat row + the interrupt note got duplicated through chat history. Reload would replay the stale `esc` row as if the user was still asking for it.

Fix: dedicated `{t:'interrupt'}` WS frame. Server handles it in `attach.js` (line 1007) by calling `session.interrupt()` directly + emitting a one-shot `interrupt-ack` note that's NOT persisted as user chat. Same path works for owners and viewers (the viewer attach branch was wired in the same commit at line 1214).

Test: `test/stop-button-interrupt.test.js` (8 cases) — verified green against current `main` at the moment of this re-close.

## Why this is being re-closed

The plan.json closure was clobbered by the MCP stale-snapshot bug the user flagged in bug-21's text (and also documented as fr-37 / fr-24): when `mcp__myco__add_plan_items` writes from a stale in-memory snapshot, it reverts any disk changes made between mycod boot and the tool call. This re-close restores the correct `done:true` state without touching code.

## Followup (not addressed here)

The MCP stale-snapshot bug is the actual durable problem. Bug-14's clobber is a symptom; td-21 + bug-7 closure metadata have been clobbered the same way. Worth filing as its own bug if not already tracked under bug-21's "Related (urgent meta-bug)" sub-mention.

meta.reason: plan.json closure clobbered by MCP stale-snapshot bug

comments.text: I just tried the stop, it didn't actually stop the task, look at the log below: run a task so that I can test stop  16:25:16 ▸ × 3 session init claude04:25 PM (interrupt sent — the in-flight Claude turn was aborted. Type your next message to continue from where the conversation left off.)  16:25:40 claude Starting a deliberately long Bash task — should run for ~90 seconds with output every 3 seconds. Click Stop at any point and the in-flight Bash should be interrupted, ending my turn with an interrupt acknowledgment. If the fix is working you'll see the "interrupt sent" note immediately on click; if it's broken, the output will keep streaming until natural completion.  16:25:42 ▸ × 2 perm asked · Bash 16:25:42 ▸ × 1 rate-limit 16:25:45 ▸ × 2 ⚠ result · 44 bytes

🔍 **Reopened** — user-confirmed Stop did NOT actually stop a real task. My 1c7ae4c fix wasn't sufficient.

## What 1c7ae4c actually did

- Routed the Stop click through a dedicated `{t:'interrupt'}` WS frame instead of the chat-text-esc path.
- Server handler calls `session.interrupt()` + emits an `interrupt-ack` chat note.
- Stopped polluting chat history with a phantom user-typed "esc" row.

## What 1c7ae4c did NOT do

`session.interrupt()` in `agent-session.js` just signals the SDK's `AbortController` + closes the streaming-input `AsyncMessageQueue`. The for-await loop on `query()` exits when the SDK observes the abort — **but only at the SDK's next yield point.** If the SDK is currently blocked inside a long-running tool (Bash sleep loop, slow WebFetch, subagent's internal API call), the abort signal arrives but the tool subprocess may continue running until natural completion, and only THEN does the iteration actually unwind.

## Existing test gap

`test/stop-button-interrupt.test.js` mocks `session.interrupt` as a counter (`interrupted++`). It verifies:
- The `{t:'interrupt'}` frame routes to `session.interrupt()` ✓
- No phantom "esc" chat row ✓
- Ack note emitted ✓
- Static guards on client + server source ✓

But it does NOT verify:
- That calling `interrupt()` on a real `AgentSession` blocked inside a tool actually unblocks the tool.
- That a Bash subprocess spawned by the SDK gets killed (vs. allowed to complete in the background).
- That no `tool_result` for the in-flight tool arrives AFTER the iteration_aborted event.
- That a follow-up user message after Stop starts a fresh iteration cleanly (not a resumed-and-retried-tool).

## Needs clarification from user (asked in chat)

Pending answers to design the right test + fix. Most likely fix shape: in `session.interrupt()`, ALSO kill any open tool subprocesses tracked by `openToolCalls`, OR escalate to `session.kill()` with a respawn-on-next-write path. The cost/benefit depends on what failure mode the user actually observed.

meta.reason: user-tested Stop did not actually stop the task; existing test mocks interrupt() and missed it

comments.text:
✅ **Fixed for real** in commit `4ed02cd` — one-character SDK option typo.

## Root cause

`server/src/agent-session.js` line 240 was passing the SDK cancellation option under the wrong field name:

```js
// WRONG (was on line 240 since the Phase 9 SDK migration):
abortSignal: this._abortController.signal,

// RIGHT (post-fix):
abortController: this._abortController,
```

The SDK's `Options` type ([sdk.d.ts:1155-1160](node_modules/@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk/sdk.d.ts)) declares:

```ts
/**
 * Controller for cancelling the query. When aborted, the query will
 * stop and clean up resources.
 */
abortController?: AbortController;
```

It's the **controller**, not the signal — and the field name is `abortController`. Passing `abortSignal: …` silently dropped the field as unknown to the SDK. `session.interrupt()` called `.abort()` on a controller that no one was listening to, the for-await loop stayed blocked inside the SDK's tool execution, and the user's 90-second Bash sleep ran to completion.

## Why this took two attempts

The 1c7ae4c fix (round 1) correctly routed the Stop click through a dedicated `{t:'interrupt'}` WS frame and called `session.interrupt()` — necessary fixes to stop polluting chat history with a phantom user-typed "esc" row. But it didn't verify the runtime contract: that the interrupt actually reaches the SDK. My test (`test/stop-button-interrupt.test.js`) mocked `session.interrupt` as a counter and asserted the routing was correct, but never checked that `sdkOpts` used the right field name. The typo on line 240 sat undetected since Phase 9.

## Test additions (in commit 4ed02cd)

Added 2 new cases to `test/stop-button-interrupt.test.js`:

1. **static guard: `sdkOpts` uses `abortController` (NOT `abortSignal`)** — locks the correct field name + has a negative grep that fails if the typo comes back.
2. **static guard: `session.interrupt()` still calls `this._abortController.abort()`** — reaffirms the 1c7ae4c invariant alongside.

Total bug-14 coverage: **10 cases** (4 in-test behavior + 6 static guards).

## Verification

- `test/stop-button-interrupt.test.js` — 10/10 green.
- Adjacent regression sweep (10 suites): **148/148 green**.

## Manual test plan for after deploy

1. Ship via `./deploy.sh` to land 4ed02cd on `myco.labxnow.ai`.
2. Ask claude to run a long Bash sleep loop (~90 sec with periodic prints).
3. Click Allow on the permission card; observe a few prints stream in.
4. Click Stop.
5. **Expected (FIXED):** Bash subprocess gets killed within ~1 sec. No more prints. `(interrupt sent — the in-flight Claude turn was aborted…)` chat note appears. The 30/30 + DONE final line does NOT appear.
6. **If still broken:** something else is in play (e.g., deploy didn't land, SDK version mismatch, container has a cached copy). Compare `docker exec myco grep -c "abortController:" /app/server/src/agent-session.js` against the expected `1` for the new field name.

items.text:
**Problem:** myco gets stuck whenever WebFetch is used on production (reported by @kkrazy 2026-05-18).

**Repro status:** Does NOT reproduce on this server. Subagent test fired `WebFetch("https://example.com")` → returned in ~3 s with ~30 byte body, clean SDK round-trip through `_canUseTool` → `_handlePermissionRequest` → `agent-event`. Historical events.jsonl across 3 prior session archives shows WebFetch listed in `system_init.tools` but ZERO actual `tool_use` events ever fired — so no past hangs in the local archives either.

**Static-analysis findings:** No WebFetch-specific code path. It uses the same `_canUseTool` flow as every other tool (Bash/Read/Edit/Grep). Permission menu emits via `_emit({type:'permission_request'...})` + `emit('menu',...)`, fanned to all attached WSes. SDK options passed: `permissionMode:'default'`, no `allowedTools`/`disallowedTools`, no WebFetch-specific settings.

**Likely root causes** (ranked by probability):
1. Permission menu doesn't render on reporter's client (CSS / device-specific) → callback promise never resolves → indefinite hang from the outside
2. Specific URL is slow/unreachable from prod server (no per-call timeout on `canUseTool`)
3. Different SDK version / settings on prod vs this server
4. Huge tool_result content jams the client browser (chrome batch with multi-MB body)

**Need from reporter to advance:**
- Which URL was being fetched
- Which server (myco.labxnow.ai / mycobeta.labxnow.ai / other)
- Was the permission menu visible in the chat pane at the time of the hang?
- Browser console errors
- If reproducible, attach output of `./collect-logs.sh --skip-mycobeta` grepped for `WebFetch|permission_request|agent-hook`

**Possible fixes once root cause confirmed:**
- `[diag-webfetch]` instrumentation (low-risk, ~30 lines, no behavior change) to capture next repro
- 30s timeout on `canUseTool` promises with auto-deny + log
- Cap `tool_result.content` at 64 KB in the wire frame (full content still in events.jsonl)

comments.text: please verify if this is still aproblem

**Closed as resolved via bug-21 fix chain.**

Verified against this session's `events.jsonl` (45 WebFetch-related events across 37 unique `tool_use` ids). Only one actual orphan permission_request exists across the entire history — the bug-21 incident at seq 3103/3104/3107/3108 (tool_use `sUN8qq3Dno` at 05:57:17). Every other WebFetch round-trip completed cleanly. The remaining "orphans" in the initial count are subagent-internal `tool_use` events that legitimately don't fire a parent-level `tool_result`.

The bug-21 fix chain (shipped, awaiting deploy) addresses the WebFetch hang root cause directly:

- **0eb1289** `bug-21`: parallel `canUseTool` no longer overwrites the menu slot (`pendingMenus` Map replaces single-slot `pendingMenu`). This was the actual symptom — two near-simultaneous permission_requests stomped each other, leaving one promise hanging indefinitely.
- **4ed02cd** `bug-14 round 2`: `abortController` typo fix — Stop now actually aborts a mid-flight WebFetch instead of letting it stream to completion.
- **72d7117** `bug-21 round 2`: `iteration_aborted` / `fatal` events terminate queue entries (so a stuck WebFetch can be cancelled out of the run-queue cleanly).
- **b243cc1** `fr-48`: dispatch marker prefix — `_activeRunItem` actually binds, so queue auto-advance fires on terminal events.

Proactive defenses listed in the original report (30 s `canUseTool` timeout, 64 KB `tool_result` cap, `[diag-webfetch]` instrumentation) are NOT shipped in this closure — they're documented as belt-and-braces for the unobserved root causes (#2, #3, #4 in the report). If WebFetch hangs recur post-deploy of the bug-21 fix chain, re-open with a fresh repro and we'll file those as a follow-up fr.

items.text:
**Problem:** myco's wire protocol (server `attach.js` ↔ client `app.js`) is ad-hoc — `JSON.stringify({ t: 'agent-event', event })` everywhere, flat event objects, no shared schema. Adding a new event type means editing the server emitter AND the client switch-statement AND remembering all the places that filter/route by `event.type`. Easy to drift.

**Source:** multica `server/pkg/protocol/{messages.go, events.go}`. Single `Message{Type string, Payload json.RawMessage}` envelope, ~13 event-category constants grouped by domain (`task:dispatch`, `task:progress`, `chat:message`, …). Decoupling envelope from typed payload lets you add events without touching the dispatcher.

**Expected:** A `server/src/protocol.js` (and matching `web/public/protocol.js`) module with:
- Event-type string constants (`AGENT_EVENT`, `CHAT`, `STATE_UPDATE`, `PRESENCE`, `MENU_PICK`, …)
- Typed payload shapes (JSDoc or TS-comment style) per event
- Helper builders: `protocol.agentEvent(ev)` returns `{ t: 'agent-event', event: ev }` etc.
- Helper decoders: `protocol.decode(json)` returns `{ kind, payload }`

**Why now (multi-user context):** With viewers + owner + multiple invited users attached to a session, the wire surface is wider than it was when myco was just owner-only. Each new frame type currently risks viewer parity bugs like `bug-13` (viewers missed agent-event for months). A typed catalog forces every new frame to be considered against every attach path.

**Effort:** ~½ day. Server side smaller (~50 lines refactor); client side bigger (~200-300 lines reducer cleanup). Tests: schema-shape tests + a regression that every WS frame the server emits matches a registered protocol type.

**Source code to study:** `multica-ai/multica` repo, `server/pkg/protocol/messages.go` and `server/pkg/protocol/events.go`.

**Problem:** myco's `attach.js` fans every `agent-event` / `chat` / `state-update` frame to every WS attached to a session, regardless of relevance. With bug-13 widening viewer fan-out, a session with 5 attached clients (owner + 4 viewers) ships every event 5×. There's no per-client subscription filter — viewers who only care about chat still receive every tool_use blob.

**Source:** multica `server/internal/daemonws/hub.go` + `server/internal/realtime/broadcaster.go`. They split daemon-facing (agent processes reporting in) from browser-facing (UIs observing) WebSockets, with scope types `workspace / user / task / chat / daemon_runtime`. Clients subscribe per-scope; broadcaster fans only to matching subs.

**Expected for myco** (revised: myco is multi-user, this is directly applicable now, not future):
- Subscription frame on attach: `{ t:'sub', scopes:['chat','agent-event','presence'] }` — viewer can omit `agent-event` if they only care about chat
- Server tracks per-WS scope set; `_broadcastTo(sessionId, scope, frame)` instead of `session.on('agent-event', onAgentEvent)` blanket subscribe
- Default scope set per role: owner=all, viewer=all (preserves bug-13 behavior), share-link=chat+presence only (privacy floor)
- Presence chip surfaces per-viewer scope set so owners can see who is listening to what

**Why it matters for multi-user myco:** Wire-volume scales with attached-client count today; scoped subscriptions make a 10-viewer session cheap instead of 10× duplicated. Also unblocks future "silent observer" modes (analytics, replay tooling) that need a subset of events.

**Out of scope:** redis/pubsub multi-node fan-out (multica has this; myco is single-host, won't need it until horizontal scale).

**Effort:** ~1 day. Net diff is moderate — touches every `session.on(...)` in attach.js plus the client's WS connect path. Tests: assert viewer with chat-only scope does NOT receive agent-event frames.

**Problem:** Today, WS event handlers in `web/public/app.js` patch in-memory state directly (`state.artifacts.byType[type] = artifact`, `_appendAgentEvent(ev)` mutates DOM). When a frame is dropped or arrives out of order across N attached clients, each client's local state drifts from server truth — exactly the class of bug that `bug-9`, `bug-11`, `ryan-blues stale-plan` were instances of.

**Source:** multica CLAUDE.md hard rule — "WebSocket events invalidate TanStack Query keys rather than writing directly to Zustand stores." Combined with workspace-scoped query-key convention. WS frames carry a `(scope, id)` tuple; the client's reaction is `queryClient.invalidateQueries([scope, id])` → React re-fetches; no in-memory patching.

**Expected for myco:**
- A small `state-cache.js` layer (no need for TanStack Query — we don't use React) that wraps the existing HTTP fetches (`/sessions/:id/artifact`, `/sessions/:id/chat/history`, `/sessions`) with cache + staleness
- WS frames trigger `cache.invalidate(key)` instead of patching `state.*` directly
- Reactive layer re-fetches + re-renders the affected pane

**Why it matters for multi-user myco:** Every attached client today does its own in-memory bookkeeping from the WS stream. When user A's tab patches `state.chatMessages` from a chat frame, user B's tab does the same — independently. Drift across clients is invisible unless someone refreshes. With invalidation-driven re-reads, server is the single source of truth, every client converges to the same view on next render.

**Trade-off:** more HTTP round-trips. Mitigated by ETag / If-Modified-Since on the artifact / chat-history endpoints (server can short-circuit unchanged responses with 304).

**Effort:** ~2 days. Touches every WS frame handler in app.js. Tests: drop a WS frame and verify the next render still matches server state.

**Problem:** myco's session/task dispatch paths (session spawn, `/run` plan-item dispatch, chat→agent forwarding) don't have an explicit "acquire capacity slot BEFORE claiming work" invariant documented or enforced. If any of these paths claims work before confirming it has the slot/budget to run it, the work piles up in a `dispatched` state with no `StartTask` follow-up — silent loss.

**Source:** multica `server/internal/daemon/daemon.go runRuntimePoller` + `handleTask`. Their code comment explicitly warns: *"acquiring the execution slot AFTER ClaimTask causes tasks to pile up in dispatched state with no StartTask."* They use a per-runtime semaphore acquired first, claim second.

**Expected for myco:**
- Audit the 3 candidate paths: `sessions.spawnSession`, `slashcmds.handleRunPlanItem`, `attach.handleChatMessage` (claude-forward branch)
- For each, identify what acts as the "capacity slot" (max-concurrent-sessions, SDK iteration in-progress, etc.)
- Reorder so slot is acquired BEFORE claim, never after
- Add a stable comment marker (e.g. `// SLOT-THEN-CLAIM (multica-pattern)`) at each enforcement site
- Static-grep test in test.sh asserting the marker exists at all known dispatch sites — catches future code that reverses the order

**Why it matters for multi-user myco:** Multiple users sharing a session = more concurrent dispatch events than the single-user case ever produced. The hazard is more likely to fire under multi-user load. Also: shared sessions mean dropped work is a worse failure (multiple users notice the loss).

**Effort:** ~½ day for the audit + comment markers + grep test. Larger if any path actually has the inverted order and needs refactoring.

**Reference:** Read multica's `runRuntimePoller` (~30 lines) for the canonical pattern.

**Problem:** Codex / Gemini / Cursor / other coding agents look for `AGENTS.md` at repo root; Claude Code looks for `CLAUDE.md`. Maintaining two files = drift risk. myco's project-spawn flow + best-practices-template.md only handle `CLAUDE.md` today.

**Source:** multica does this. Their `AGENTS.md` is a one-liner: `# See CLAUDE.md`. Other-agent users get the same instructions for free, with zero duplication or sync risk.

**Expected:**
- Add `AGENTS.md` at the myco repo root with `# See [CLAUDE.md](./CLAUDE.md) for project instructions.`
- Update `injectBestPracticesIntoClaudeMd` in `server/src/sessions.js` to ALSO create/maintain `AGENTS.md` redirect file in spawned project dirs (idempotent: if AGENTS.md exists with non-redirect content, leave it alone)
- Add a static-grep test: `test -f AGENTS.md` exists at repo root + content matches the redirect pattern

**Why it matters for myco specifically (multi-agent context):** myco's sessions are agent-native, and the auto-injected CLAUDE.md gives consistent guidance. Right now that consistency is Claude-only. As users mix agents — running Codex in one session, Claude in another — AGENTS.md ensures the non-Claude tools see the same project-shaped rules without us maintaining two templates.

**Effort:** ~10 min for the repo-root file + commit. Optional second pass (~½ hour) for the per-project mirror in sessions.js + test.

**Out of scope:** Maintaining a fully-separate AGENTS.md template with agent-tool-neutral phrasing. The CLAUDE.md content is already mostly agent-tool-neutral (just says "the agent" not "Claude" in most rules); a redirect is sufficient until that breaks down.

**Problem:** myco dual-writes session state — `/data/sessions.json` (registry, chat, allow/deny) AND `<project>/_myco_/events.jsonl` (agent-event log). They desync on crashes; plan-item updates, chat appends, run-outcomes each have their own persistence path with no single durable order.

**Source:** [OpenHands](https://github.com/All-Hands-AI/OpenHands). Every state change = typed `Event` (Action vs Observation) appended to `EventLog`. In-memory state = FOLD over the log; restart = re-fold from snapshot + tail.

**Expected for myco:**
- New `server/src/event-store.js`: `append(type, payload)` + `fold(handlers)` + `snapshot(every N)`
- ALL state mutations route through it: chat, plan-item edit, allow-list update, run-outcome
- `sessions.json` becomes a derived projection rebuilt on startup (or from periodic snapshot)
- Crash recovery = replay log from last snapshot deterministically

**Why for multi-user myco:** Two clients editing simultaneously (owner adds td-N while viewer comments on bug-M) can race the sessions.json write today. Append-only log serializes naturally; fold layer resolves conflicts.

**Files touched:** `agent-session.js` (1042 lines, emit via store), `sessions.js` (~700, loadStore/saveStore L66-79 becomes projection), `artifacts.js` (plan/test/arch route through store). One-shot migration script reads existing dual-write state, rebuilds unified log.

**Effort:** L (~2 days). Tests: kill server mid-write, restart, assert state matches last appended event.

**Problem:** Chat messages in myco are atomic envelopes — a tool result streaming over 10s re-renders the whole bubble each time. Reconnecting viewers see partial state as fully-replaced rows, losing scroll position. Tool input + tool result + assistant text flatten into one agent-event blob with no per-part id for partial updates.

**Source:** [OpenCode (sst)](https://github.com/sst/opencode). Their `messages` + `parts` model: messages are envelopes; "parts" (text-part, tool-call-part, tool-result-part, status-part) are independently streamable atoms, each with stable id. Server broadcasts part-by-part; client patches in place by id.

**Expected for myco:**
- Typed envelope carrying `parts: [{ id, type, ... }]`
- Part types: `assistant-text`, `tool-call`, `tool-result`, `status`, `error`
- Server emits `{ t:'part-add'|'part-update'|'part-done', sessionId, msgId, part }` instead of monolithic agent-event
- Client renderer keys by `part.id` — incremental updates patch the right DOM node
- Viewer reconnect: server replays missing parts via `?afterPartSeq=N` (extends existing `?afterSeq`)

**Why for multi-user myco:** Multiple viewers watching a slow tool call see whole-bubble re-renders out of order today. Part-keyed patches converge to identical DOM across all attached clients without race.

**Depends on:** `fr-27` (typed wire protocol). Ideally land them together so new part frames are catalogued from day one.

**Files touched:** `agent-session.js` (split tool_result into per-part fragments), `attach.js` (new `t:'part-*'` frame family), `app.js` (~8077 lines, chat-bubble renderer becomes part-keyed).

**Effort:** L (~3 days). Tests: part arrives out of order → final DOM still correct.

**Problem:** When a user wants to "try a different approach" mid-session (e.g. revert before the last 3 turns + explore a different prompt), the only path is to start a new session — losing context, chat history, plan items, workspace edits. No native fork / checkpoint mechanism.

**Source:** [opcode (winfunc)](https://github.com/winfunc/opcode). Tauri 2 / React desktop GUI for Claude Code with branching checkpoint timeline (`src-tauri/src/checkpoint/`). User snapshots at turn N, forks, switches branches, diffs any two checkpoints.

**Expected for myco:**
- New `server/src/checkpoints.js`: `snapshot(sessionId, turnId, label?)`, `fork(sessionId, ckId)`, `list(sessionId)`, `restore(sessionId, ckId)`, `diff(sessionId, ckA, ckB)`
- Storage: `<project>/_myco_/checkpoints/<turn>/` containing truncated `events.jsonl` snapshot + git-stash of workspace tree (use git plumbing, not new VCS)
- Forked session = sibling session id sharing checkpoint pointer; events diverge into the new session's events.jsonl
- UI: timeline gutter in chat pane with Fork / Restore / Diff actions

**Why for multi-user myco:** When multiple users collaborate on a session and someone wants to explore an alternative without disrupting others, fork lets them branch off without losing shared history. Useful for /run exploration of multiple plan-item approaches.

**Files touched:** new `checkpoints.js` (~200 lines); `sessions.js` (+ `parentSessionId` + `forkedAt` fields); `agent-session.js` (honor `forkedFromCheckpoint` on spawn); `app.js` (timeline gutter UI). New routes: `POST /sessions/:id/checkpoint`, `POST /sessions/:id/fork/:ckId`, `GET /sessions/:id/checkpoints`, `GET /sessions/:id/diff?a=&b=`.

**Effort:** L+ (~4 days; biggest piece is git-worktree integration for the workspace side). Tests: snapshot → mutate → restore → assert state matches snapshot.

**Problem:** myco's `permissions.js`, `myco-mcp.js`, and other modules hold process-global state (registries, in-flight tool calls, allow-list caches) implicitly shared across all concurrent sessions. As multi-user concurrency grows, shared-mutable state is a footgun: a permission update in session A can race a permission check in session B; the MCP server's plan-item appender uses a session-id closure but the surrounding module is singleton.

**Source:** [Goose (block)](https://github.com/block/goose). They migrated from a global `AgentRef` to a per-`session_id` `AgentManager` with own ExtensionManager / ToolMonitor / channels. [Discussion #4389](https://github.com/block/goose/discussions/4389) documents the motivation: eliminate global lock contention when many sessions run concurrently.

**Expected for myco:**
- Introduce `class SessionContext` in `agent-session.js` carrying: `sessionId`, `permissionsCache`, `mcpServer`, `toolMonitor`, per-session event bus
- Refactor `permissions.js` (~180 lines) to export `createPermissionsForSession(sessionId)` factories instead of singleton methods
- Refactor `myco-mcp.js` (~152 lines) so per-session closure becomes a SessionContext member, not closure-captured variable
- Audit other modules for hidden globals: `summarizer.js`, `transcript.js`, `slashcmds.js`

**Why for multi-user myco:** Today a permission allow-list mutation in session A's chat could be observed mid-check by session B if they share in-memory cache. Per-session isolation prevents whole class of cross-session leakage. Cheap to do BEFORE concurrency grows; painful after (every change backported into per-session shape).

**Effort:** M (~1.5 days). Tests: spawn 5 concurrent SessionContexts, mutate permissions in each, assert no cross-contamination.

**Problem:** Users repeatedly run the same multi-step workflows by hand — "kick off N-thread architecture review," "extract patterns from project X via WebFetch + summarise into _myco_/research/," "file a bug + write regression test first per BP §5 + commit." These workflows live in tribal knowledge or ad-hoc chat prompts; they're not shareable, not parameterised, not versioned.

**Source:** [Goose (block)](https://github.com/block/goose). "Recipes" = YAML files containing: prompt template + required extensions + parameters + retry policy. Subrecipes nest as isolated sub-sessions whose summaries return to parent orchestrator. Portable, version-controlled, composable.

**Expected for myco:**
- New `_myco_/recipes/*.yaml` per-project recipe dir (auto-injected like CLAUDE.md)
- Recipe shape:
  ```yaml
  name: arch-review
  params: [target_branch]
  steps:
    - subagent: general-purpose
      prompt: "Review {target_branch} for X"
      verify: "output contains 'Recommendations:'"
  ```
- Slash command `/recipe <name> [params]` in `slashcmds.js` (~1042 lines) to invoke
- Subrecipes nest as isolated sub-sessions (via Agent tool); summaries return to parent's chat
- New Plan-tab subview: "Recipes" — list / run / view past runs
- Plan-item integration: a plan item can declare a recipe to run on `/run`

**Why for multi-user myco:** Recipes are inherently team artifacts — one user authors, all users (and agent on /run dispatch) can invoke. Multi-user becomes the WHY: single-user setup has less need to crystallise workflows; team setup amplifies leverage.

**Files touched:** new `recipes.js` (~200 lines); `slashcmds.js` (/recipe handler); `sessions.js spawnSession` (seed `_myco_/recipes/` on spawn); new UI subview in `app.js`.

**Effort:** M+ (~2 days). Tests: invoke recipe with params, verify subagent fires, verify summary returns to parent chat.

**Problem:** When `web/public/best-practices-template.md` is updated (e.g. §7+§8 in `ccb5625`+`103bc88`), the new content reaches ONLY new projects spawned after the deploy + the Arch-tab UI banner on browser refresh. Existing projects whose CLAUDE.md already contains `<!-- myco-best-practices-start -->` KEEP the old template forever — the agent reads stale content on every turn across full deploys.

**Root cause:** `injectBestPracticesIntoClaudeMd` in `server/src/sessions.js:514` short-circuits `if (existing.includes(BP_SENTINEL_START)) return;` to protect user hand-edits. Side effect: zero update propagation.

**Expected:**
- Stamp each injection with `<!-- myco-bp-hash:<sha256-of-template> -->` inside the block
- On call, if block exists: compare embedded hash vs current template hash
- Match → no-op
- Differ → re-inject new template, preserving text bracketed by NEW inner markers `<!-- myco-bp-custom-start -->` ... `<!-- myco-bp-custom-end -->` where teammates put hand-edits
- Fresh injections embed BOTH markers so users have a documented place for additions

**Files:** `server/src/sessions.js` (~30 lines added); `web/public/best-practices-template.md` (header note about custom-marker pair); `test/best-practices-inject.test.js` (drift triggers re-inject, custom block survives, matching-hash no-ops).

**Migration:** existing CLAUDE.md files lack new markers — first post-deploy attach treats missing hash as drift, re-injects with markers + current content. Idempotent thereafter.

**Effort:** ~½ day. Strict superset of current behavior.

**Payoff:** §7+§8 shipped this session won't reach existing projects without this. Future template edits propagate automatically on next attach.

**Problem:** Ad-hoc messages to Claude can trigger code/functionality changes without an approved work item gating them.
**Expected:** All code changes must be triggered by an approved td/fr/bug.
**Actual:** Arbitrary chat messages to Claude can initiate code or functionality changes with no approved td/fr/bug backing them.

meta.originalText: all code changes should be triggered by an approved td/fr/bug. No adhoc msg to claude should trigger any code/functionality change.

items.text:
**Problem:** Session owners have no way to delegate management responsibilities, forcing all admin actions through a single user.
**Expected:** Owner can assign an admin role that grants the same permissions as the owner, except deletion of the session.
**Actual:** Only the owner holds elevated permissions; no admin role exists.

items.text:
**Problem:** New session dialog has no way to specify a repo URL, so the session workspace can't be auto-populated from a project's GitHub/Gitee URL.
**Expected:** New session dialog accepts a repo URL (GitHub/Gitee); on submit, the session workspace is populated from that URL.
**Actual:** No repo URL field exists; workspace must be set up manually.

items.text:
**Problem:** td/fr/bug item history is truncated after the worked-on item is finished, losing the full record of what happened.
**Expected:** Full history/summary is saved as a comment on the item that was worked on.
**Actual:** History is truncated post-completion, so the item retains only a partial record.

items.text:
**Problem:** Granting admin via `/admin` doesn't actually elevate the user — labxnow was made admin but still can't send messages to claude (admin/owner-only).
**Expected:** After `/admin <user>` succeeds, that user gains admin permissions and can send messages to claude.
**Actual:** Bot confirms "is now an admin on this session", but labxnow still sees the read-only viewer notice ("claude-routing is owner-only") and can't message claude.
**Context:** Repro log — `/admin claude` → "✓ is now an admin on this session. Inherits everything except delete-session + grant/revoke admin"; labxnow then blocked with "(read-only viewer — claude-routing is owner-only…)".

items.text:
**Problem:** AskUserQuestion popups aren't routed to the user who started the operation and aren't broadcast across their logged-in devices, blocking other users on the same session.
**Expected:** The popup appears for the initiating user and is broadcast to all devices where that user is logged in.
**Actual:** The popup is not consistently delivered to the initiator or mirrored across their devices, so other users on the shared session get blocked.

meta.originalText: askuserquestion pop up should appear to the user started the operation and make sure its broadcasted to all devices the user logged in. Otherwise it will block other users working on the same session.

So the workflow is write code on local machine and sync to remote server for execution and debug. This feature will collect information about remote machine, generate ssh private key and ask user to add it to remote host. collect information about where to install the code, how to install, how to run the program. Then instruct claude code to use the remote envrionment when needed.
