items.text: Plan item ID wraps in mobile HUD as time ticker grows

meta.originalText: leave enough space for hud display in mobile mode so that the plan item id doesnt wrap as the time ticker grows.

items.description:
**Problem:** In mobile mode, the HUD doesn't reserve enough space for the time ticker, causing the plan item ID to wrap as the ticker widens.
**Expected:** HUD layout reserves sufficient width for the growing time ticker so the plan item ID stays on a single line.
**Actual:** As the time ticker grows, it squeezes the plan item ID and forces it to wrap.

runs.summary: Root cause: the HUD row uses flex with justify-content: space-between. Inside, .hud-task-title-wrap (also flex) holds .hud-task-id + .hud-task-text, while .hud-task-status holds #hud-duration-text + the Stop button. As #hud-duration-text grew from '1s' to '12345s', the right-side .hud-task-status widened and the left-side .hud-task-title-wrap squeezed its children. .hud-task-text was already protected by `white-space: nowrap + overflow: hidden + text-overflow: ellipsis + max-width: calc(100vw - 140px)` (the mobile override from bug-45), but the .hud-task-id chip had NO protection — once the ticker hit 3-4 digits on a 375px-wide phone, the chip wrapped to a second line. Fix is surgical: added `flex-shrink: 0` + `white-space: nowrap` to .hud-task-id so the chip keeps its natural width and never breaks lines. The squeeze still hits .hud-task-text first (designed to truncate); the ID badge stays intact. Both desktop and mobile inherit the new behavior since the base .hud-task-id rule sits outside the @media (max-width: 600px) block — universally the right thing for a chip badge. Regression test: test/bug-51-hud-id-no-wrap.test.js (3 cases) — asserts .hud-task-id declares flex-shrink: 0, declares white-space: nowrap, and a bug-51 marker comment exists near the rule. Wired into test/test.sh after fr-81 r1. Adjacent suites stay green: bug-45-mobile-hud-layout (26/26), bug-39-action-row-uniform-height (5/5). Not yet committed/pushed/deployed — awaiting user direction.

items.description:
**Problem:** Critic only runs once after the full pipeline finishes, so bad analyze/code/verify outputs propagate downstream before any feedback.
**Expected:** Critic kicks in at each stage — analyze, code, and verify — gating progression on its review.
**Actual:** Critic runs only as a final pass after everything is finished.

Committed locally as `7860936`. Local is 1 commit ahead of origin. Three options:

1. **Push + deploy now** — local tests pass, the change is well-scoped (server-only, no client surface), and Gemini's 503 is a Google-side issue unrelated to the change quality.
2. **Wait + retry Gemini in ~10 min** then push + deploy if ✓ AGREED.
3. **Hold here** — commit sits locally until you decide.

Say "push and deploy" or similar — recommendation is option 1 since this fix specifically makes critic responses BETTER in the long run (more context = fewer INSUFFICIENT INFORMATION false-flags), and the Gemini outage is exactly the kind of thing the existing ↻ Retry button is designed to handle from the UI.

**✓ AGREED** with substantive reasoning, on the very first attempt with the simulated r2 enrichment. This is itself a self-validating result — Gemini gave a fuller, more confident verdict precisely because the prompt now includes the full file context (51,758 chars vs the typical ~10k for a diff-only fire).

**The structural answer to your question** is worth flagging as a real myco limitation:

> **In-product critic only fires on the session that runs the dispatched work.**
> When you dispatch `[run:plan#X]` on session A while the actual code change happens in session B (different container, different developer, different timeline), the critic gate on A never sees the code change — A's claude sat idle, no `turn_result` fires.

This is the same class of issue as the dispatch-label-drift bug fixed earlier today (which addressed *false positives* — the critic firing on wrong WIP). The mirror image is **false negatives** — the critic *not firing at all* on legitimate work that happens out-of-session.

Three credible fixes (none shipped today; this is a structural gap):

1. **`/critique <commit-sha>`** slash command — explicitly invoke the critic on an arbitrary commit's diff. Solves the out-of-session case cleanly.
2. **Dispatch-aware code review on push** — when origin/main advances with a plan-item ID mentioned in any commit message, auto-fire the critic on those commits. Like a PR check.
3. **Manual ↻ Critique button on plan items** — same as Retry but for "I want to critique whatever the most recent code change associated with this item is."

For *right now* on td-33 r2: my manual Gemini invocation (with simulated r2 enrichment) returned ✓ AGREED with a 51k-char prompt — the largest meaningful critic invocation we've done this session. That's the closest substitute for the missing in-product verdict.

meta.originalText: other than the general purpose critic, add a separate critic focusing Test validity ensuring behaviour correctness and another critic on potential perf and security issue. Design the prompt in a way maximize prefix caching

items.description:
**Problem:** A single general-purpose critic conflates concerns and misses domain-specific issues in tests, performance, and security.
**Expected:** Two additional critics alongside the general one: (1) a Test-Validity critic that verifies behavioural correctness of tests, and (2) a Perf/Security critic that flags performance regressions and security risks. Prompts are structured so the shared/static prefix is maximized for prefix caching.
**Actual:** Only the general-purpose critic exists; no targeted coverage for test correctness or perf/security, and prompt layout is not optimized for cache reuse.
**Context:** Prompt design should place stable system/instruction content first and variable inputs (diff, target code) last to maximize prefix-cache hits across critic invocations.

Found the root cause. It's three things compounded — and it's a real gap in the §9 methodology I just landed, not just a "you didn't see it" thing.

**The actual flow during fr-95:**

1. You dispatched `[run:plan#fr-95]` → `session._activeRunItem` was set ✓
2. My **first reply** (analyze stage) emitted `[stage: analyze done]`. The sentinel handler at `attach.js:190` fired correctly — but analyze had **ZERO source edits** (by the directive I just wrote). So `changedInfo.entries.length === 0` → log line `[td-33] stage-done(analyze) — no dirty files; skipping critique`. **No critic UI.**
3. My first reply ended with `turn_result subtype=success`. `attach.js:361` ran: **`session._activeRunItem = null`**.
4. Every subsequent turn — code-stage edits, verify-stage test writing, the full-suite runs — happened with `_activeRunItem` already null.
5. For all those later stage-done sentinels: handler at `attach.js:193` hit `if (!active || !active.itemId) ... return` → log `[td-33] stage-done(code) fired without active run item — skipping`. **No critic UI.**
6. The **final** critique gate (`attach.js:298`) also gates on `active.itemId`. Same issue — by the time meaningful diffs accumulated, `_activeRunItem` was null. **No critic UI.**

**So three reasons compounded:**

| Stage | Why no critic fired |
|---|---|
| **analyze** | By design — zero source edits, no diff to critique |
| **code** | `_activeRunItem` was already cleared after the analyze-stage `turn_result` |
| **verify** | Same — multiple `turn_result`s had passed |
| **final** | Same — `_activeRunItem` null on every subsequent `turn_result` |

**The architectural gap I unintentionally introduced:**

The §9 3-stage methodology I just landed assumes the stage-done sentinels reliably fire critiques. But the gating in `attach.js:190-209` + the `_activeRunItem = null` clear at line 361 means:

> **The 3-stage critic methodology only works if all three stages happen WITHIN A SINGLE agent turn.**

items.text: Critic-popover dismisses on outside-click + Esc, no recovery path. User-reported (verbatim): 'the critic popover is not modal, click outside of it made the popover disappear and no way to bring it back again'. Pre-fix: bug-50 r2 wired backdrop+Esc dismissal for `isError || isIntermediate` states with the intent of giving stuck users an 'escape hatch' — but the explicit Dismiss button already provides that path, and once dismissed `state.critiqueReview = null` wipes the verdict (no way to recall it). Fix: remove the safeToDismissByBackdrop branch entirely. The popover becomes truly modal in all states; dismissal is only via the explicit buttons (Dismiss / Discard / Accept / etc.). Eliminates the accidental-dismiss footgun on intermediate/error verdicts.

Intermediate (stage-checkpoint) verdict pane is missing the ✓ Accept button. Today the intermediate state shows only ↻ Retry + ✓ Dismiss; user needs an explicit ✓ Accept affordance at each stage checkpoint so they can signal 'this checkpoint is good, proceed to next stage.' Without it, the auto-pause-await-accept methodology (§9 directive) has no per-stage accept path — dismiss is semantically wrong (means 'continue waiting for final critique'), retry is for re-firing the critic. Fix: add ✓ Accept Stage to the intermediate actions row. Click handler clears local state + fires critique-resolved broadcast (reason='accept-checkpoint'); cross-device sync via bug-54 wiring already in place. Related: bug-54 just shipped the broadcast surface this leverages.

_activeRunItem cleared on first turn_result, breaking the 3-stage critic methodology. Pre-fix attach.js:361/381 sets session._activeRunItem = null on EVERY turn_result, but the §9 3-stage methodology has stages spanning multiple turns (analyze → user accept → code → user accept → verify). The stage-done handler at attach.js:193 short-circuits when _activeRunItem is null, so critic never fires on stages 2 + 3 — the user reported empirically: 'how come i didn't see critic kicked off during implement of fr-95'. Fix: don't clear _activeRunItem on turn_result. Instead clear it when (a) [stage: verify done] sentinel arrives + final critique broadcasts, OR (b) an explicit /rundone slash command fires, OR (c) the run-queue advances to a different plan item (covers Discard case). The runQueue advancement logic that depends on the current clear-on-turn_result needs revisiting too — today it advances after EVERY turn_result; with the fix it advances only after verify-stage-accept or fr-96 stage-machine transition. Foundational — bug-56 and fr-96 both depend on this.

Server-side per-plan-item stage state machine. Each plan item carries { stage: 'analyze'|'code'|'verify', status: 'in_progress'|'awaiting_verdict'|'awaiting_accept'|'asked_to_fix'|'accepted' }. Stage transitions: in_progress → awaiting_verdict on [stage: X done] sentinel; awaiting_verdict → awaiting_accept on critic verdict broadcast; awaiting_accept → (next stage in_progress | asked_to_fix | accepted) based on user action; asked_to_fix → in_progress (redo same stage). Item-complete when verify stage = accepted. HUD displays current stage + status. Persists in rec.artifacts.plan.items[].meta.stageState. Broadcasts state-update kind:'plan-item-stage' on transitions for cross-device sync. Depends on bug-57.

td-33 r4: update §9 directive in web/public/best-practices-template.md to codify the full stage state machine. Replace the current 'auto-iterate up to 2x' clause with the user's spec: 1) stage → 2) stage critic → 3) next stage IF ACCEPTED (no auto-advance, pause for explicit accept signal) → 4) rerun critic if follow-up question provided → 5) rerun stage if asked-to-fix (back to step 2 once stage result is provided). Three stages per plan item: analyze, code, verify. Accept signal: clicking ✓ Accept on verdict pane OR chat message expressing accept (accept/yes/looks good/✓/etc). Silence ≠ accept. Depends on bug-57 + bug-56 + fr-96 (the implementation that makes the directive deliverable).

items.text: Stage critic verdict doesn't actually pause the agent — subsequent stage sentinels overwrite the modal before the user can review. User-reported (verbatim): 'the analyze stage verdict didn't pause the process, it eventually get overriden by the final stage overall verdict popover.' Root cause: fr-96's state machine is OBSERVABLE (status transitions to awaiting_accept) but not ENFORCED. Nothing server-side stops claude from emitting the next [stage: X done] sentinel, and the client's critique-review WS handler replaces state.critiqueReview unconditionally on each broadcast. Fix: (1) server enforcement — when stageState.status === 'awaiting_accept' for the active run-item, the stage-done handler defers / drops subsequent sentinels until the user signals accept-stage / fix-stage; (2) client guard — if state.critiqueReview is already showing an unresolved intermediate verdict, do NOT replace it with a new critique-review broadcast (paired safety net for race conditions). This finally makes the §9 pause-and-await-accept methodology physically real instead of directive-only.

items.text: Specialty critic findings don't drive the overall hasDisagreement — if Test Validity or Perf/Security flags a real gap, the verdict still says '✓ AGREED' because only the General critic's verdict gates. User-reported: a verdict where Test Validity critic clearly identified TWO missing assertions (fileApiPreamble called with 'viewer' tier; readOnly: true wiring based on ctx.access) but the overall verdict still showed Agreed. Two paired fixes: (1) specialty prompts (test-validity.js, perf-security.js) must explicitly require either '✓ AGREED' or '✗ DISAGREE' on the first line so detection is unambiguous — remove the 'your verdict is INFORMATIONAL' framing that signals to the model 'your finding doesn't really matter'. (2) critique.js aggregation: hasDisagreement = NOT (generalIsAgreed AND testValidityIsAgreed AND perfSecurityIsAgreed). Any specialty disagreement flips the overall verdict + pauses the run queue + surfaces the Discard/Fix/Accept buttons. Inversely, '✓ AGREED' on all three is required to silently advance.

Server-side §9 injection at session spawn — universal directive propagation. Pre-fix: §9 directive lives ONLY in web/public/best-practices-template.md, rendered into Arch tab UI but NEVER reaches the per-session CLAUDE.md that claude actually reads. When a session clones a repo, the clone's CLAUDE.md (which lacks §9) wins; per-session CLAUDE.md templating doesn't fire on cloned-repo sessions. Empirically confirmed via myco3 session debugging: cloned upstream kkrazy/myco.git CLAUDE.md had NO methodology references; claude emitted ZERO stage sentinels across ~280 events; the deployed bug-61 hard gate was inert (nothing to gate). Fix: extend the spawn flow to inject the current best-practices-template.md §9 block into the per-session CLAUDE.md regardless of upstream content. Idempotent — check for `td-34` marker before injecting; skip if already present. This makes the methodology truly universal across ALL myco-managed projects, not just ones whose upstream CLAUDE.md happens to include it. Companion to the immediate fix (Option A — add §9 to this repo's CLAUDE.md directly); Option B is the architectural fix.

Final critique on turn_result overwrites an unresolved intermediate verdict — bug-61's gates are incomplete. User-reported (verbatim): 'before the first stage (analyze) stage verdict is accepted, the overall verdict is also popped up. the process should be paused until an verdict is accepted.' Root cause: bug-61 server gate only checks stageState in the stage-done handler (attach.js:190); the FINAL critique on turn_result success fires from a separate code path (attach.js:298 IIFE) that doesn't check stageState. Symmetrically, bug-61's client guard exempts `!msg.isIntermediate` (finals) so the client doesn't block replacement either. Empirically observed on myco4 (cloned kkrazy/myco.git post Option A so the per-session CLAUDE.md has §9; claude IS emitting [stage: X done] sentinels). The model still continues all 3 stages in one turn; bug-61 drops the 2nd + 3rd intermediate sentinels (good), but the final critique on turn_result fires + overwrites the analyze verdict. Fix: (1) server-side — in attach.js's turn_result success IIFE, before calling triggerGeminiCritique with the final intent, check stageState; if status is awaiting_verdict/awaiting_accept for an intermediate stage, DEFER the final critique (store on rec) instead of firing. When the user resolves the intermediate (via /critique/resolve with accept-stage/fix-stage OR clearActiveRunItem with reason discard), fire any pending deferred final. (2) client-side — extend bug-61 critique-review WS handler so a FINAL broadcast does NOT replace an unresolved intermediate verdict either; instead queue or buffer it. Paired with the server fix, this is the race-safety net.

Critic verdicts are generic QA review, not problem-solving validation against the plan-item. User-reported: 'the verdict must criticize if the proposed solution actually solves the fr/td/bug we are working on. right now it's just a general review of the diff, the analyze result and whether it solves the problem of the fr/td/bug should be our focus.' Root cause: the basePrompt in critique.js framing claude as 'elite, independent QA and security auditor' biases the critic toward generic code-quality review; the userPrompt does include `Task to accomplish: ${item.text}` but it's BELOW the diff (not primary). Specialty prompts (general/test-validity/perf-security) each focus on their domain without elevating 'does this solve the plan-item' as the central question. Stage-specific gap: analyze stage has ZERO diff (per the directive) so generic-diff-review doesn't even apply — critic should evaluate the PLAN against item.text; code stage should evaluate whether the diff IMPLEMENTS the solution; verify stage should evaluate whether the regression test SPECIFICALLY catches the user-reported failure mode (would it have red-flipped against the pre-fix code?). Fix: (1) rewrite basePrompt to put 'does claude's work solve the user's plan-item' as the PRIMARY criterion + state secondary criteria; (2) restructure userPrompt to put item.text + the problem framing AT THE TOP, then diff/code as supporting evidence; (3) add stage-aware prompt addenda — analyze stage critic gets 'evaluate the plan against the problem', verify stage critic gets 'would this test have caught the original failure'. Specialty prompts (general/test-validity/perf-security) inherit the stage-aware framing too.

## Stage: analyze — bug-66

### Restating the bug in my own words
The session record (`rec` in `/data/sessions.json`) has a single `mainProject` slot that's meant to anchor `_myco_/` (plan.json, critic.md, events.jsonl, diagrams). The user is reporting two coupled symptoms:

1. **Multiple main projects can accumulate per session** — i.e. nothing in the system prevents a session from ending up with more than one git checkout sitting under `absCwd`, and the "+ add" path doesn't enforce that the existing `mainProject` is the only one.
2. **Plan + memory paths drift away from `<mainProject>/_myco_`** — because resolution still falls back to legacy auto-detect (`findProjectRoot` scans sibling subdirs for `.git/`) and `migrateMainProjectIfNeeded` *bails out* on multi-candidate workspaces…

✓ success · 411.9s · $4.3538 · 30↓/27751↑

### Summary of code-stage diff

**`server/src/artifacts.js`**
- `findProjectRoot` — removed the legacy sibling-subdir auto-detect scan (the "drift cause"). Resolution rules are now {`mainProject` set + dir exists → that path; `mainProject` set + dir missing → `null` + loud warn; `absCwd` IS a checkout → `absCwd`; else `null`}.

items.description:
**Problem:** Sessions can accumulate multiple "main" projects when added via `+`, and project memory/plan paths are not consistently anchored under `main-project/_myco_`.
**Expected:** Each session has exactly one main project. Plan is read from `main-project/_myco_/plan.json`, and `main-project/_myco_` is the canonical root for project memory.
**Actual:** Adding via `+` permits multiple main projects per session, and plan/memory locations drift away from `main-project/_myco_`.
**Context:** Paths involved — `main-project/_myco_/plan.json`, `main-project/_myco_/` (project memory root).

## Stage: analyze — bug-66

### Restating the bug in my own words
The session record (`rec` in `/data/sessions.json`) has a single `mainProject` slot that's meant to anchor `_myco_/` (plan.json, critic.md, events.jsonl, diagrams). The user is reporting two coupled symptoms:

1. **Multiple main projects can accumulate per session** — i.e. nothing in the system prevents a session from ending up with more than one git checkout sitting under `absCwd`, and the "+ add" path doesn't enforce that the existing `mainProject` is the only one.
2. **Plan + memory paths drift away from `<mainProject>/_myco_`** — because resolution still falls back to legacy auto-detect (`findProjectRoot` scans sibling subdirs for `.git/`) and `migrateMainProjectIfNeeded` *bails out* on multi-candidate workspaces, leaving the resolution to be done at every read by the alphabetical-first scanner. That's the "drift" — same session, two reads, two potential answers.

### What I found in the code
- The only `+` button is `#btn-spawn` (web/public/index.html:34) → opens the spawn modal → `doSpawn()` (web/public/app.js:2860) → `POST /sessions` (server/src/index.js:597-625) → `spawnSession` (server/src/sessions.js:888). Spawn-modal flow is already required-gated by fr-94 Phase 3 r1.
- `findProjectRoot(rec)` (server/src/artifacts.js:162) honors `rec.mainProject` when set + the dir exists; **otherwise it auto-scans sibling subdirs** for `.git/` and returns the alphabetical-first hit. That is the "drift surface" — every plan/memory read on a no-mainProject session can land in a different sibling project than the user expected.
- `migrateMainProjectIfNeeded` (artifacts.js:235) only commits a value when there's **exactly one** candidate; multi-candidate sessions stay unmigrated forever and re-scan every time.
- `rec.mainProject` is written in only one place: `spawnSession` at sessions.js:949. There is **no `/sessions/:id` PATCH or "add another project" endpoint** today — nothing client-facing lets the use

### Summary of code-stage diff

**`server/src/artifacts.js`**
- `findProjectRoot` — removed the legacy sibling-subdir auto-detect scan (the "drift cause"). Resolution rules are now {`mainProject` set + dir exists → that path; `mainProject` set + dir missing → `null` + loud warn; `absCwd` IS a checkout → `absCwd`; else `null`}.
- Added `setMainProject(rec, name)` — the single chokepoint for `rec.mainProject` writes. Throws on already-set-different-value (single-main invariant), empty name, missing absCwd, or missing target dir. Idempotent same-name re-set is a no-op.
- `migrateMainProjectIfNeeded` — multi-candidate branch is now deterministic (picks alphabetical-first + persists via `setMainProject`) instead of bailing with a warning. Single-candidate branch also routed through `setMainProject` for consistency.
- Exported `setMainProject` (top-level + `__test`); also added `migrateMainProjectIfNeeded` to `__test` so the new test can exercise it.

**`server/src/sessions.js`**
- `spawnSession` seed write at line 949 now calls `artifacts.setMainProject(record, mainProject)` via lazy-require instead of stamping the field directly. Wrapped in try/catch so a failure logs but doesn't abort the spawn (the directory was just created moments earlier; failure here is a defensive log point, not a user-facing block).

**`test/bug-66-single-main-project-anchor.test.js`** — new file, 19 cases:
- 6× chokepoint behaviour (`setMainProject`)
- 4× `findProjectRoot` resolution (including the bug-66 "no sibling fallback" guard — the test that would have caught the original drift)
- 4× deterministic migration (`migrateMainProjectIfNeeded`)
- 2× canonical anchor witness (`artifactFilePath` lands at `<absCwd>/<mainProject>/_myco_/plan.json`, or `null` — never a sibling)
- 3× chokepoint-adoption static-grep guards (sessions.js calls it, no direct writes, setMainProject is exported)

items.description:
**Problem:** The verdict panel doesn't persist consistently across devices or after session restart, unlike AskUserQuestion.
**Expected:** Verdict panel renders using the same mechanism as AskUserQuestion, so it remains consistent across device and session restart.
**Actual:** Verdict panel uses a different rendering path that doesn't survive device switches or session restarts.

runs.result:
---

## Stage: analyze — fr-98

### Restating the problem in my own words
The verdict panel that pops up after a `[stage: X done]` sentinel — showing the critic's verdict + the Accept Stage / Fix Stage / Ask Critic buttons — is **fire-once and ephemeral**. It only reaches devices that were attached at the moment of broadcast. If you switch devices, attach a second device, or the server restarts, the pane stays empty even though the per-item state machine (fr-96 `stageState`) still says `awaiting_accept` — so the run is paused waiting for input you can no longer see or give.

`AskUserQuestion` (claude's permission/clarifying-question modal) doesn't have this problem because it's tied to an SDK tool_use event that lives in `session.buffer` + `events.jsonl`. Every new attach replays the buffer via the `agent-replay` WS frame, so the menu re-renders identically on every device, after every restart.

You want the verdict panel to behave the same way: as long as the verdict hasn't been resolved, a fresh attach (new device or post-restart) should see the same pane the originating device saw.

### What I found in the code

**AskUserQuestion's working persistence pattern:**
- `server/src/agent-session.js _handleAskUserQuestion` — the SDK's tool_use event lands in `session.buffer` (the per-session event ring) + mirrors to `<absCwd>/_myco_/events.jsonl`.
- `server/src/attach.js _shipAgentReplay` (line 1200) — on every attach (initial OR catch-up via `?afterSeq=N`), reads `session.buffer` and ships an `agent-replay` WS frame. The client re-renders every tool card.
- Cross-device: same `session.buffer` → identical replay on every device.
- Container restart: `events.jsonl` feeds `session.buffer` reload.

**Verdict-review's broken path:**
- `server/src/critique.js:451` — `session.emit('state-update', { kind: 'critique-review', itemId, hasDisagreement, isError, isIntermediate, stage, critique, diff, criticName, criticId, specialties })`. **Fire-once `state-update` broadcast.** Not

- **Accept** → I move to code stage with Approach A.
- **B instead** → pivot to literal `session.buffer` push.
- **⚡ Ask Claude to Fix Stage** → tell me what's substantively missing and I'll redo.

Paused awaiting your accept signal. If the critic verdict comes back ✓ AGREED again and you accept, I move to code stage with Approach A as outlined. If there's a substantive concern that didn't surface in the prior two critic passes, name it and I'll address it directly.

### Summary of code-stage diff

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `server/src/stageState.js` | + `setLastCriticReview(item, payload)` / `clearLastCriticReview(item)` / `getLastCriticReview(item)` — three helpers next to existing stageState API. Persisted at `item.meta.lastCriticReview` in plan.json. Exported top-level. |
| `server/src/critique.js` | Captured the broadcast payload as `broadcastPayload`, then after the `session.emit('state-update', …)` fires, calls `setLastCriticReview(item, payload)` + `sessionsMod.saveStore()` (skipped on error verdicts to mirror the existing `_transitionStageState` skip). In `resolveCritique`, the accept-stage / fix-stage branches now also call `clearLastCriticReview(item)` so resolved verdicts don't replay. |
| `server/src/attach.js` | `_clearAndBroadcastStageState` (the single chokepoint that calls `clearStageState`) now also calls `clearLastCriticReview` and saves if either changed. `_sendAttachSnapshot` scans `artifacts.plan.items` for any item with a persisted verdict AND `stageState.status ∈ {awaiting_verdict, awaiting_accept}`, ships it as `{ t:'state-update', kind:'critique-review', …payload, _replayedOnAttach:true }`, capped at 1 per attach. |
| `test/fr-98-verdict-panel-persistence.test.js` | New file. 13 cases covering: helper round-trip, payload preservation, lifecycle coupling (stageState + lastCriticReview move together), and 5 static-grep wiring guards across stageState.js, critique.js, attach.js. |
| `test/test.sh` | + `node_test_result` line for fr-98. + `test_lastCriticReview_paired_with_stageState` static guard in `run_static_checks` (±10-line window — synthetic violation self-tested to fail; clean tree passes). |
| `test/fr-96-plan-item-stage-state-machine.test.js` | Widened the post-emit window from 1500 → 3000 chars in the "transition to awaiting_accept" case. The fr-96 ordering contract is unchanged; the legitimate fr-98 persist block sits between the emit and the existing transition. |

items.description:
**Problem:** After the user accepts a stage, the workflow stalls instead of progressing to the next stage automatically.
**Expected:** Accepting a stage (via "accept" / "proceed" / "verify stage") immediately advances to the next stage (e.g., verify stage runs `./test/test.sh` + wire-check + grep regression sweep).
**Actual:** Workflow waits idle until the user sends an additional "continue" message.
**Context:** Repro covered by `test/test.sh` — bug-60 wired next to `plan-run-comment.test.js`. Post-fix verification: bug-60 13/13 ✓, plan-run-comment 4/4 ✓ (was 1/4), plan-item-run-button 13/13 ✓. Related to CLAUDE.md §9 step 3 (silence ≠ accept).

meta.originalText: for each plan item submitted for implementation, it is suppose to be separated into 3 stages: 1. analyze, with a proposal to solve the problem,  2. code, implment the proposed solution 3. verify to ensure the proposed solution fix the problem. after each stage there should be a critic stage where we will involve a selected critic model to ensure each stage is doing it's job. the critic should bring up a modal popover for user to 1. review the solution 2. ask further question 3. accept the solution 4. ask claude address the issues. only proceed to next stage when previous stage solution is accepted.

items.description:
**Problem:** Plan items submitted for implementation don't run through the required staged workflow with critic gating, so solutions advance without review.
**Expected:** Each plan item runs 3 stages — 1. analyze (propose a solution), 2. code (implement the proposal), 3. verify (confirm the fix). After every stage a selected critic model runs and opens a modal popover letting the user (1) review the solution, (2) ask further questions, (3) accept the solution, or (4) ask Claude to address the issues. The next stage only starts once the prior stage's solution is accepted.
**Actual:** Stages are not separated, no critic step runs between them, and no acceptance modal gates progression.
